title: Eridantes utibilis Crosby & Bishop 1933

Dupérré (CPAD); Baie­James (Jamésie): 122 km NNE LaSarre [49°48’N, 78°54’W] 06.–15.vi.1997, Malaise/flight interception trap (pan) in regenerating Black spruce forest, 1Ψ, P. Paquin & N. Dupérré (CPAD); Baie­James (Jamésie): 122 km NNE LaSarre [49°48’N, 78°54’W] 09.ix.2002, Berlese extraction from regenerating Black spruce forest, 1Ψ, P. Paquin & N. Dupérré (CPAD); Baie­James (Jamésie): 119 km N LaSarre [49°49’N, 79°01’W] 04.vii.1997, Berlese extraction from mature Black spruce forest, 5Ψ, P. Paquin & N. Dupérré (CPAD); Baie­James (Jamésie): 119 km N LaSarre [49°49’N, 79°01’W] 09.ix.1997, Berlese extraction from mature Black spruce forest, 2Ψ, P. Paquin & N. Dupérré (CPAD).   Diagnosis.—  Males of  Eridantes utibilis  are characterised by a large, curved embolus (E) with a bent tip (Fig. 25), the shape of the embolic division (arrow, Fig. 25) and the finger­like palpal tibial apophysis (PTA) (Fig. 27). Females are recognised by the presence of a pale median triangular region (arrow) and the rounded spermathecae (S) flanked on each side (Fig. 28).   Distribution.—  Known from Québec (present records) and Nova Scotia to Wisconsin and New York (see Buckle  et al . 2001).   Remark.—  The numerous records from the boreal forest of Québec suggest that it may be locally common in conifer forests, especially among mosses and litter. Interestingly, most collections were done with Berlese extraction of litter. Other trapping methods in the same location were not as productive.
